title: Mary Wollstonecraft
summary: English writer and philosopher who made the foundational argument for women's rational equality in A Vindication of the Rights of Woman (1792), challenging the Enlightenment's failure to extend its own principles to half the human race — and whose unconventional life became as contested as her ideas.
hook: Wollstonecraft argued that women were not naturally inferior to men — they had simply been denied education and treated like slaves. She wrote this in 1792. The argument was obvious once made and resisted for another century and a half. The gap between the argument's logic and the world's response is the history of feminism.

beginner.theFigure:
In 1792, Mary Wollstonecraft published a book arguing that women only seemed less capable because they were denied an education. One famous critic called her a hyena in petticoats. At the time, almost every educated person in Europe assumed women were naturally less rational than men. Mary Wollstonecraft's book, A Vindication of the Rights of Woman, said that was wrong. Women seemed less rational only because they had been denied education.

Wollstonecraft was an English writer who lived from 1759 to 1797. She grew up poor and had to work for most of her life. She taught school, worked as a governess, and translated books for money. She was not wealthy or connected. She wrote her most famous book in about six weeks.

She also lived in ways that shocked people around her. She had a child without being married. She attempted suicide twice. She eventually married the writer William Godwin but kept her own name. She died at thirty-eight, just after giving birth to her second daughter. That daughter grew up to write Frankenstein.

For most of the century after her death, people remembered her for her personal life more than her ideas. Her ideas were too threatening, and her life made it easy to attack her. Only in the twentieth century did she come to be seen as a founding figure of feminist thought.
beginner.worldOfIdeas:
Wollstonecraft wrote during a remarkable period in history. In the 1780s and 1790s, revolutions were happening in America and France. People were arguing that human beings had natural rights. The key idea was that reason, the ability to think and judge, was what gave people rights.

But almost everyone who made this argument meant men. The French Revolution declared liberty and equality in 1789 but excluded women from political life. One French woman, Olympe de Gouges, wrote her own declaration demanding rights for women. She was executed in 1793.

Wollstonecraft was part of a group of radical thinkers in London who supported the French Revolution. They believed that reason could improve society. She agreed with them, but she pushed further than they were prepared to go. If reason is what gives people rights, she asked, and women can reason, why do they not have rights?
beginner.centralIdea:
Wollstonecraft's main argument was about education. From childhood, girls were trained to be pleasant, obedient, and attractive. They were taught to please men, not to think for themselves. This kind of upbringing produced weak and dependent adults. Then men pointed to that weakness as proof that women could not be trusted with rights or education.

Wollstonecraft said this was circular thinking. Women were weak because they had been kept weak. If girls were given the same education as boys, they would develop the same reasoning abilities. The problem was not female nature. The problem was female training.

She also made a second point. The way men treated women was bad for everyone. Men who kept their wives childlike and dependent were creating exactly the silliness and vanity they then complained about. Better education for women would make women better people, and that would make families and society better too.
beginner.howSpread:
When the Vindication was published, many people read it. Some praised it. Many attacked it. Then something unusual happened. Wollstonecraft died in 1797. Her husband quickly published a memoir about her life. He meant well. But the book revealed her illegitimate child, her suicide attempts, and her unconventional relationships.

Critics used this information to attack her ideas. They said her argument for rational equality had been made by a woman who could not even manage her own life. This unfair tactic worked for a long time. For about a hundred years, her personal life overshadowed her ideas.

The movement for women's voting rights, which grew in the nineteenth century, eventually looked back to Wollstonecraft. But many suffragists did not use her argument. They preferred to say women deserved the vote because they were morally superior to men, not because they were rational equals. Wollstonecraft's harder, more logical argument had to wait for the feminism of the 1960s and 1970s to be fully recovered.
beginner.contestedInheritance:
Some later feminist thinkers have asked whether Wollstonecraft's argument was limited in an important way. She wanted women to be admitted to the world of reason and rights. But that world had been built by men, for men. Was she accepting men's values on men's terms rather than questioning whether those were the right values?

There is also a question about class. Wollstonecraft mostly wrote about middle-class women. Her vision of education assumed time and money. Working-class women and enslaved women were not really part of her argument, even though she was aware of the abolitionist movement of her day.

These are real limitations. They do not cancel her achievement, but they mean her argument was a starting point, not a complete answer. The feminism that came after her had to extend and sometimes challenge the foundations she laid.

general.theFigure:
A small detail from 1798 captures something essential about Mary Wollstonecraft's fate. Her husband, the philosopher William Godwin, published a memoir of her life four months after she died in childbirth. He meant it as a tribute. It disclosed her illegitimate daughter, her two suicide attempts, and her relationships outside marriage. Within months, journals that had reviewed A Vindication of the Rights of Woman respectfully turned hostile. The argument was now associated with a scandalous life, and that life was offered as evidence against the argument. Wollstonecraft spent the following century more famous for her biography than her philosophy.

This was not just bad luck. It was a pattern that worked for a long time. For roughly a hundred years after her death, Wollstonecraft served as a warning rather than an inspiration. The use of a thinker's personal life to discredit her ideas is a tactic that has a name now: the biographical fallacy. In her case, it held for generations. The recovery of her work by twentieth-century feminism was a genuine recovery, not a continuation of a tradition that had stayed alive.
general.worldOfIdeas:
The immediate target of the Vindication was Jean-Jacques Rousseau, whose widely read Emile (1762) had set out an account of how girls should be educated. Rousseau's ideal girl, Sophie, was to be raised to please, defer, and serve. Wollstonecraft's response was sharp: this was not a description of female nature but a method for producing female weakness. Rousseau had designed a system that generated the incapacity he then cited as proof that women should be excluded from rational life.

She was also writing in the wake of the French Revolution's failure to include women in its declaration of rights. Olympe de Gouges had published her own declaration demanding rights for women in 1791 and was executed in 1793. The Vindication appeared in 1792, between those two events. Its argument was not abstract but aimed at real political choices being made in Paris and London at that moment.
general.centralIdea:
The Vindication makes its case at several levels. At the core is a claim about reason. Enlightenment philosophers had argued that the capacity for rational thought is what entitles people to rights. Wollstonecraft accepted this premise and then turned it against the system. Rational capacity is not a male trait. Women possess it too. What looks like female irrationality is the result of an education designed to suppress it.

This is also a practical argument about reform. If women were educated to think rather than to charm, they would show the capacities they were assumed to lack. Wollstonecraft was not claiming that women and men were identical. She was claiming that the specific capacity at stake, rational judgment, was not sex-specific.

She added a social argument beyond individual rights. The system of female dependence corrupted everyone involved. Men who treated women as decorative children got women who behaved as decorative children, then complained about female vanity. Reforming women's education was not just about women. It was about the character of society as a whole.
general.howSpread:
The suffrage movement's relationship with Wollstonecraft was complicated by the movement's own choices. Many suffragists in the nineteenth and early twentieth centuries argued for the vote on the grounds of women's moral superiority rather than rational equality. The claim was that women's domestic virtues would improve corrupt political life. This argument went down better with Victorian audiences, but it rested on a different logic from Wollstonecraft's. It tended to exclude working-class and non-white women from the frame while accepting assumptions about gender difference that Wollstonecraft had challenged.

The historian Barbara Taylor has shown that Wollstonecraft's full rehabilitation came only with the feminism of the 1960s and 1970s. That movement needed a founding philosophical argument for rational equality, not just a moral case for female virtue. It found what it was looking for in the Vindication. Even then, the recovery was selective. Second-wave feminism tended to highlight her rationalism and pass over the religious and emotional dimensions of her thought.
general.contestedInheritance:
A recurring debate about Wollstonecraft asks whether her rationalism challenged Enlightenment assumptions or simply accepted them. The critique, raised by several feminist thinkers since the 1980s, is that Wollstonecraft wanted women admitted to a man's world without questioning whether that world's values were right. She took reason as the measure of human worth. She did not ask why reason had been prized above feeling, or why feeling had been dismissed as lesser to begin with.

The biographer Janet Todd documented the emotional intensity of Wollstonecraft's own life, her passionate friendships and difficult relationships, and asked what connection this has to the cool rationalism of the Vindication. Virginia Woolf posed a sharper version of the question: was Wollstonecraft a thinker who happened to live dramatically, or was her life itself a demonstration that a woman could exist as a full human being outside the conventions that confined her? Historians still disagree.

There is also a debate about class and race. The Vindication focuses on middle-class women. Its vision of female education assumes time and resources that most women, in Britain and across the empire, did not have. Some scholars argue that a truly universal claim about rational equality would have required engaging with slavery and colonial rule. Wollstonecraft was aware of both but kept them at the margins of her argument.

advanced.theFigure:
The challenge Wollstonecraft poses to how historians have read her is the challenge of the founder whose founding was only recognized retrospectively. Unlike Locke or Rousseau, whose influence was continuous and acknowledged, Wollstonecraft was substantially forgotten between her death and her twentieth-century recovery. This means that the Wollstonecraft available to feminist movements was partly a construction — different periods recovered different aspects of her work and life in response to their own needs.

Cora Kaplan's work has been most attentive to these constructions. Her essay 'Wild Nights: Pleasure/Sexuality/Feminism' — collected in Sea Changes (1986) — argues that the recuperation of Wollstonecraft as a rationalist pioneer suppressed the sexual and emotional dimensions of her thought and life that were as central to her project as the philosophical argument. The sanitized Wollstonecraft of the standard feminist canon was in some ways a falsification.
advanced.worldOfIdeas:
Wollstonecraft's engagement with Rousseau has been analyzed in detail by Sylvana Tomaselli, who shows that the Vindication is not simply a rejection of Rousseau but a more complex engagement: she accepted many of his premises about the relationship between social corruption and natural goodness while rejecting his application of those premises to women. This makes the Vindication a more philosophically intricate text than the standard rationalist reading allows — it is a critique of Rousseau conducted partly on Rousseau's own terms.

The religious dimension of Wollstonecraft's thought — recovered by Barbara Taylor — is equally important and equally underplayed in secular readings. Wollstonecraft's rationalism was not Enlightenment deism but something closer to rational religion — the belief that reason and revelation were complementary rather than opposed, and that the God of nature had endowed human beings, male and female, with rational capacities that it was both a moral and a religious obligation to develop. This framework gives the Vindication a different character from the purely secular rights argument it is usually read as.
advanced.centralIdea:
The most philosophically significant element of Wollstonecraft's argument is its relationship to Kantian moral philosophy, which was being developed simultaneously. Kant's argument that moral agency required rational autonomy — the capacity to give oneself the moral law rather than receiving it from external authority — has the same structure as Wollstonecraft's argument that women's exclusion from rational development was a denial of their moral agency. Whether she was aware of Kant's work is uncertain; the parallel is genuine.

This parallel has been developed by Christine Korsgaard and other Kantian philosophers who have argued that Wollstonecraft's critique is not merely a historical document but a philosophical argument that Kantian ethics is committed to endorsing on its own terms. The argument is: if moral agency requires rational autonomy, and rational autonomy requires development and education, then any social system that denies rational development to a class of persons is not merely unjust but self-undermining — it produces people who cannot be moral agents in the full sense the theory requires.
advanced.howSpread:
The twentieth-century recovery of Wollstonecraft proceeded through two distinct channels: the scholarly and the popular. The scholarly recovery — associated with the publication of a collected edition of her works in the 1970s and with the scholarly work of figures including Barbara Taylor, Janet Todd, and Lyndall Gordon — reconstructed the full range of her work and situated it in its historical context. The popular recovery — associated with second-wave feminism's search for founding figures — produced a somewhat different Wollstonecraft: more heroic, more straightforwardly rationalist, more cleanly aligned with contemporary feminist politics.

The tension between these two recoveries has been productive. Scholarly attention to the complexity, contradictions, and historical specificity of Wollstonecraft's thought has complicated the feminist mythology without displacing it. The result is a figure who is more interesting than the founding mother myth allows and more consequential than the specialist might insist.
advanced.contestedInheritance:
The most consequential contemporary debate about Wollstonecraft concerns her relationship to what is sometimes called 'imperial feminism' — the use of arguments about women's rights to justify colonial interventions that claim to liberate women from 'backward' cultures. Whether Wollstonecraft's universalist rationalism is implicated in this tradition is contested. Scholars including Uday Mehta have argued that Enlightenment liberalism's universalism was always compatible with colonialism in ways that liberal theory did not acknowledge; critics respond that Wollstonecraft's own position was more nuanced than this charge allows.

The relationship between Wollstonecraft's feminism and race is a related concern. The Vindication is almost exclusively concerned with white middle-class women; slavery and racial hierarchy barely appear, despite Wollstonecraft's awareness of abolitionist arguments. Whether this silence is a fundamental limitation or a historically understandable focus continues to be debated in feminist scholarship.

foundation.theStory: Mary Wollstonecraft made the foundational argument for women's rational equality in 1792 and was largely forgotten for a century because her unconventional life made her ideas easy to dismiss. The recovery of her work by twentieth-century feminism is itself a historical lesson: intellectual history is not simply the history of ideas but the history of whose ideas get preserved, transmitted, and recognized.
foundation.theDebate: Scholars debate whether Wollstonecraft's rationalism accepts masculine values on their own terms rather than challenging them; how her emotional life relates to her philosophical argument; the religious dimensions of her thought that secular readings have suppressed; and her relationship to class and race — whether the Vindication's focus on middle-class women is a fundamental limitation or a historical constraint.
foundation.whyItMatters: Wollstonecraft matters as the first systematic philosophical argument for women's rational equality — the founding text of feminist political philosophy. She matters also as a case study in how radical ideas are transmitted or suppressed: her personal life was used to discredit her argument for a century, and the argument had to be recovered rather than simply inherited.
questions: Wollstonecraft argued that women's apparent inferiority was produced by education and social conditioning, not natural difference. Is this argument still the right foundation for feminist politics, or does it accept too much of the Enlightenment's framework?

Wollstonecraft's personal life — unconventional relationships, illegitimate child, suicide attempts — was used to dismiss her ideas for a century. What does this tell us about the relationship between a thinker's biography and the reception of their ideas?

interpretation.scholarlyConversation: Wollstonecraft scholarship divides between philosophical readings (Tomaselli on her relationship to Rousseau, Korsgaard on the Kantian dimensions), biographical readings (Todd, Gordon, Taylor), and feminist theoretical readings (Kaplan on the suppression of her emotionalism and sexuality). Taylor's Mary Wollstonecraft and the Feminist Imagination is the most sophisticated recent synthesis. The religious dimension she recovers has not been fully integrated into the standard accounts. The debates about class and race are increasingly central to feminist scholarship on Wollstonecraft.
interpretation.evidenceAndLimits: The primary evidence is rich: the Vindication, her other publications, her letters, and Godwin's memoir. The letters are particularly important for recovering the emotional and personal dimensions that the philosophical texts suppress. The limits: Wollstonecraft's personal papers were partly destroyed; her voice in some key relationships is available only through others' accounts. The recovery of her working-class contemporaries' perspectives on her arguments — women who were not her audience — is largely impossible.
interpretation.takingAPosition: A strong scholarly argument about Wollstonecraft must engage with both the philosophical argument and the historical context — with what she argued and what she could have argued given her situation. Arguments that celebrate her as a straightforward champion of universal equality miss the class and racial limitations of her framework. Arguments that dismiss her as complicit in liberal imperialism undervalue the genuine radicalism of her intervention in the context of the 1790s.
questions: Wollstonecraft's Vindication is sometimes criticized for accepting masculine rational values rather than challenging them — for wanting women to be admitted to a man's world rather than questioning the world's terms. Is this a fair criticism? What would a genuinely transformative feminist philosophy look like?

Virginia Woolf asked whether Wollstonecraft's life was itself her argument — the demonstration that a woman could live as a full human being. How should we think about the relationship between a thinker's life and their philosophy?

research.topic: The religious dimension of Wollstonecraft's rationalism
research.content: Barbara Taylor's scholarship has established that Wollstonecraft's rationalism was not secular Enlightenment deism but a form of rational religion — the conviction that God had endowed human beings with rational capacities that it was a moral and religious obligation to develop. This dimension was suppressed in the secular feminist recovery of the twentieth century. The religious framework gives the Vindication a different character from the purely secular rights argument it is usually read as, and connects it to the dissenting Protestant tradition in which Wollstonecraft was formed.

research.topic: The impact of Godwin's memoir on Wollstonecraft's reception
research.content: Historical scholarship has documented that William Godwin's Memoirs of the Author of A Vindication of the Rights of Woman (1798) — published four months after her death — dramatically damaged the reception of her ideas by disclosing her illegitimate daughter, her suicide attempts, and her relationships outside marriage. Contemporary reviews that had been broadly positive became hostile. The memoir's impact on the subsequent century of Wollstonecraft reception is documented in the history of the suffrage movement's ambivalent relationship to her legacy.

research.topic: The nature and content of Wollstonecraft's early relationships
research.content: The emotional and intellectual content of Wollstonecraft's formative relationships — particularly her intense friendship with Fanny Blood and her early experiences of domestic service — cannot be recovered in detail. What evidence exists: letters, primarily to Fanny Blood and later to Gilbert Imlay, that document the emotional intensity of her attachments. What cannot be recovered: the reciprocal dimensions of these relationships, the full content of conversations and exchanges that shaped her thinking, and the experience of her years as a governess and teacher before the Vindication.

comparativeNarrative.name: Suffragist Perspective
comparativeNarrative.content: The nineteenth-century suffrage movement had an awkward relationship with Wollstonecraft. Many suffragists argued for the vote on different grounds than she did. Rather than claiming that women were rational equals, they argued that women were morally better than men and would clean up corrupt political life. This went down better with Victorian audiences. But it came at a cost. The moral-superiority argument left working-class women and non-white women largely out of the picture. It also accepted the idea that women were different from men in kind, which was exactly what Wollstonecraft had challenged.

comparativeNarrative.name: Second-Wave Feminist Recovery
comparativeNarrative.content: The feminism of the 1960s and 1970s needed a founding text. It found one in the Vindication. What the movement wanted was a clear argument that women are rational equals, and Wollstonecraft had made that argument in 1792. But the recovery was selective. The emotional and spiritual sides of her thinking were largely set aside. So was the question of class. The Wollstonecraft that second-wave feminism recovered was shaped by what that movement needed: a champion of rational equality and professional rights. The harder, more contradictory figure behind the text was less visible.

comparativeNarrative.name: Intersectional and Postcolonial Critique
comparativeNarrative.content: More recent feminist scholarship asks about the limits of what Wollstonecraft argued. Her Vindication focused on middle-class women. She said very little about slavery or race, even though she knew about the abolitionist movement of her time. Some scholars argue that her claim to speak for all women was not as universal as it appeared. They point out that the same logic used to justify empire claimed to bring reason and civilization to people judged as lacking both. Wollstonecraft did not endorse empire, but her framework did not challenge it either. The question scholars now debate is whether this was a personal limitation or a deeper gap in her thinking.

rabbitHole.label: Feminism
rabbitHole.reason: Wollstonecraft's Vindication is the founding text of feminist political philosophy — the argument from which subsequent feminist theory and politics developed.

rabbitHole.label: The Enlightenment
rabbitHole.reason: Wollstonecraft wrote within and against Enlightenment liberalism — her argument was that the Enlightenment had failed to apply its own principles consistently to women.

rabbitHole.label: Rousseau
rabbitHole.reason: The Vindication is primarily a polemic against Rousseau's account of female education in Emile — understanding Rousseau is essential for understanding what Wollstonecraft was arguing against.

rabbitHole.label: Simone de Beauvoir
rabbitHole.reason: De Beauvoir's The Second Sex (1949) is the twentieth century's equivalent of the Vindication — the systematic philosophical argument for women's equality that Wollstonecraft began.

rabbitHole.label: Gender
rabbitHole.reason: Wollstonecraft's argument that female weakness was socially produced rather than naturally given is the founding move in the modern analysis of gender as a social construction.

rabbitHole.label: The French Revolution
rabbitHole.reason: The French Revolution's exclusion of women from the rights it proclaimed was the immediate context for the Vindication — Wollstonecraft's argument was partly a response to the Revolution's failure of consistency.

reference.author: Wollstonecraft, Mary
reference.title: A Vindication of the Rights of Woman
reference.annotation: The essential primary source — read it first. Short enough to read in an afternoon and still remarkable for its clarity and anger. A modern edition with scholarly introduction is recommended.

reference.author: Taylor, Barbara
reference.title: Mary Wollstonecraft and the Feminist Imagination
reference.annotation: The most sophisticated recent scholarly account — Taylor recovers the religious and emotional dimensions of Wollstonecraft's thought that purely rationalist readings had suppressed. The essential starting point for serious engagement.

reference.author: Todd, Janet
reference.title: Mary Wollstonecraft: A Revolutionary Life
reference.annotation: The essential biography — Todd's account of Wollstonecraft's life is thorough, sympathetic without being hagiographic, and attentive to the relationship between the life and the philosophy.

reference.author: Gordon, Lyndall
reference.title: Vindication: A Life of Mary Wollstonecraft
reference.annotation: The most readable accessible biography — Gordon's narrative account of Wollstonecraft's life is accessible to general readers without sacrificing accuracy or nuance.

reference.author: Tomaselli, Sylvana
reference.title: Wollstonecraft: Philosophy, Passion and Politics
reference.annotation: The essential philosophical account — Tomaselli's analysis of Wollstonecraft's engagement with Rousseau is the most rigorous treatment of the philosophical dimensions of the Vindication.

reference.author: Kaplan, Cora
reference.title: Sea Changes: Essays on Culture and Feminism
reference.annotation: Contains the essential feminist theoretical reading of Wollstonecraft — Kaplan's argument that the rationalist recovery suppressed the sexual and emotional dimensions of Wollstonecraft's project remains important.

reference.author: Rousseau, Jean-Jacques
reference.title: Emile, or On Education
reference.annotation: Rousseau's account of female education (the ideal girl Sophie, raised to please and defer) is the immediate target of the Vindication; the entry narrates its contents as the system Wollstonecraft set out to refute.

reference.author: Godwin, William
reference.title: Memoirs of the Author of A Vindication of the Rights of Woman
reference.annotation: Godwin's posthumous memoir, by disclosing Wollstonecraft's illegitimate child, suicide attempts, and unmarried relationships, is the document the entry credits with wrecking her reception for a century; the entry's account of that reversal draws directly on it.

reference.author: Korsgaard, Christine M.
reference.title: Creating the Kingdom of Ends
reference.annotation: Korsgaard's Kantian constructivism supplies the framework the entry uses to argue that Wollstonecraft's critique is one Kantian ethics is committed to endorsing on its own terms — that denying rational development to a class of persons is self-undermining.

reference.author: Mehta, Uday Singh
reference.title: Liberalism and Empire: A Study in Nineteenth-Century British Liberal Thought
reference.annotation: Mehta's thesis that Enlightenment liberalism's universalism was always compatible with colonialism is the specific argument the entry invokes when weighing whether Wollstonecraft's universalist rationalism is implicated in 'imperial feminism'.

reference.author: Woolf, Virginia
reference.title: Mary Wollstonecraft (in The Common Reader: Second Series)
reference.annotation: Woolf's essay frames the interpretive question the entry attributes to her — whether Wollstonecraft's life was itself her argument, the demonstration that a woman could live as a full human being.

commerce.title: A Vindication of the Rights of Woman
commerce.author: Mary Wollstonecraft
commerce.note: Read the primary source first — it's short, fierce, and still remarkable. The Oxford World's Classics edition with Tomaselli's introduction is the best starting point.

commerce.title: Vindication: A Life of Mary Wollstonecraft
commerce.author: Lyndall Gordon
commerce.note: The most accessible biography — Gordon's narrative is compelling and accurate, the best entry point for readers who want the life before the philosophy.

commerce.title: Mary Wollstonecraft: A Revolutionary Life
commerce.author: Janet Todd
commerce.note: The essential scholarly biography — more thorough than Gordon and essential for understanding the full range of Wollstonecraft's life and work.

commerce.title: Mary Wollstonecraft and the Feminist Imagination
commerce.author: Barbara Taylor
commerce.note: The essential scholarly account of her thought — Taylor's recovery of the religious dimension transforms how the Vindication should be read.

commerce.title: Wollstonecraft: Philosophy, Passion and Politics
commerce.author: Sylvana Tomaselli
commerce.note: The most rigorous philosophical account — Tomaselli's analysis of Wollstonecraft's engagement with Rousseau is essential for understanding the philosophical stakes of the Vindication.

commerce.title: The Wrongs of Woman — Mary Wollstonecraft (1798, posthumous)
commerce.author: Mary Wollstonecraft
commerce.note: Her unfinished novel — published posthumously — shows a Wollstonecraft exploring the emotional and sexual dimensions that the Vindication's rational framework had constrained. Essential for a complete picture.

popularCulture.title: Frankenstein — Mary Shelley (1818)
popularCulture.description: Wollstonecraft's daughter wrote Frankenstein. The link is real: she grew up reading her mother's books. Some scholars ask whether the novel is a response to her mother's trust in reason — a daughter's worry about what happens when science outstrips wisdom. The novel gets the danger right. It does not settle whether the target is reason itself or only reason misused.

popularCulture.title: The 'founding mother' construction
popularCulture.description: Wollstonecraft is often called feminism's founding mother. The label gets something right: she made the first serious philosophical case for women's equal rights. What it distorts: the real Wollstonecraft was angrier, more contradictory, and harder to fit into a tidy origin story. Movements need founders, and the myth tends to smooth out the edges that make her interesting.

popularCulture.title: Wollstonecraft in contemporary political language
popularCulture.description: When politicians argue that women's apparent disadvantages are caused by society, not nature, they are using Wollstonecraft's core idea without knowing it. The argument has been used so widely that its origin has become invisible. That is partly a sign of success: ideas that work get absorbed into common sense and lose their author.

popularCulture.title: Mary on a Pedestal — Maggi Hambling (2020)
popularCulture.description: In 2020, a bronze statue of Wollstonecraft was put up in London. It shows a small nude figure rising from a mass of swirling female forms. Critics said it was wrong to depict a women's rights champion as a nude. The sculptor said she was showing an everywoman, not a portrait. The argument about the statue is itself a sign of how disputed Wollstonecraft's legacy remains.

popularCulture.title: The biographical fallacy — life used to dismiss ideas
popularCulture.description: After her death, critics attacked Wollstonecraft's ideas by pointing to her personal life: an illegitimate child, two suicide attempts, unmarried relationships. For over a hundred years, this tactic worked. It is now called the biographical fallacy — using a person's life to discredit their argument. Her case is the clearest example of this move in intellectual history, and it held for generations.

associatedWorks: A Vindication of the Rights of Woman
A Vindication of the Rights of Men
Letters Written During a Short Residence in Sweden, Norway, and Denmark
